content.text: Counting Tokens in Claude: Methods & Best Practices
Counting tokens tells you how many tokens your prompt will use before sending it to Claude, helping you manage costs, stay within context limits, and make informed decisions about your prompts
Ensure your prompt fits within 200k tokens (or 1M for Opus)
Choose appropriate model for prompt size
lets you count tokens in a message without actually creating it. It supports:

vo = anthropic.Client()
total_tokens = vo.beta.messages.count_tokens(
    messages=["Sample text"],
    model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
)
Current token usage in your context window
Token usage for current session with cost estimates
: View token consumption, costs by model, and usage over time in

curl -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $(gcloud auth print-access-token)" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8" \
  -d @request.json \
  "https://LOCATION-aiplatform.googleapis.com/v1/projects/PROJECT_ID/locations/LOCATION/publishers/anthropic/models/count-tokens:rawPredict"
: Runs prompts, images, and PDFs against the official Anthropic token counting API
: Quick manual counting without API code
: Reads Claude's local JSONL files for token metrics and usage analysis
Real-time token consumption and burn rate tracking
- Real-time token count for current conversation - Mini progress bar against 200k context limit - Intercepts Claude's API responses
If you don't have access to the API:

estimated_tokens = len(text) / 4  # characters
estimated_tokens = num_words * 1.33  # words
for contractions, punctuation, or special characters
What Gets Counted in Token Counts
Every message in conversation history takes tokens, so as messages grow slightly larger, the
After sending a prompt, the API response includes:
Different models may tokenize the same text differently
Always specify the model when counting (e.g.,
Images are encoded as tokens (not just bytes)
PDFs are extracted and tokenized as text
You Can't See Individual Token IDs
You need to inspect network traffic or use custom tokenizers to see raw IDs
Practical Workflow: How to Count Tokens
If >200k tokens (or model limit), reduce content
Consider caching for repeated context (system prompts, codebase)
Use Chrome extension for real-time progress bar
Review Claude Console for historical usage
to control context window and costs
for repeated context (system prompts, large documents) to reduce token costs
: Don't exceed 60–70% of context window to avoid losing earlier tokens
or Claude Console for historical analysis
Understanding token counting is essential for managing costs, optimizing prompts, and avoiding context limit surprises.
When working with counting tokens, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with counting tokens. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of counting tokens scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
